items.title: Definition and anatomy
items.contentHtml:
Definition: infection of orbital tissues

  • Orbital cellulitis is a suppurative infection of the orbital contents posterior to the orbital septum, which differentiates it from preseptal cellulitis.
  • It affects fat, extraocular muscles and nerves; the key anatomical barrier is the orbital septum (extension of the periosteum).
  • Pathophysiology: direct spread from paranasal sinuses (most common ethmoid) or by hematogenous route (in children <5 years).

items.title: Etiology and route of spread
items.contentHtml:
Main cause: acute ethmoidal sinusitis

  • Acute ethmoidal sinusitis is the most common cause (60-80% of cases), due to the thinness of the lamina papyracea that separates the ethmoid sinus from the orbit.
  • Other causes: penetrating trauma, orbital surgery, dacryocystitis, dental infection (via maxillary sinus).
  • In children, the hematogenous route is relevant (associated with bacteremia due to Haemophilus influenzae type b before the vaccine).

items.title: Microbiology
items.contentHtml:
Most common germ: Streptococcus pneumoniae

  • The most common germ is Streptococcus pneumoniae, followed by Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Streptococcus pyogenes.
  • In patients with chronic sinusitis or immunocompromised patients, consider anaerobes. (Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium) and gram-negative bacilli.
  • In unvaccinated or asplenic children, Haemophilus influenzae type b remains relevant.

items.foco: Key symptom: proptosis with ocular limitation
items.title: Semiology and proptosis
items.contentHtml:
Key symptom: proptosis with ocular limitation

  • Proptosis (exophthalmos) is the cardinal sign, accompanied by painful limitation of ocular motility (chemosis, eyelid edema).
  • Axial proptosis suggests medial subperiosteal abscess (due to ethmoid sinusitis); non-axial proptosis may indicate abscess in another quadrant.
  • Evaluate with exophthalmometry (normal <20 mm, asymmetry >2 mm is pathological).

items.title: Warning and serious signs
items.contentHtml:
Alarm sign: ophthalmoplegia

  • ophthalmoplegia (paralysis of extraocular muscles) indicates involvement of cranial nerves III, IV or VI due to inflammatory extension or apical abscess.
  • Other alarm signs: decreased visual acuity, relative afferent pupillary defect (RPD), corneal hypoesthesia (V1 branch of the trigeminal).
  • The presence of ophthalmoplegia is an indication for urgent imaging (CT with contrast) and surgical evaluation.

items.title: Key differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Difference of preseptal cellulitis: without proptosis

  • Preseptal cellulitis affects tissues anterior to the orbital septum; it does not produce proptosis or limitation of ocular motility.
  • In preseptal: eyelid edema, erythema, fever; Visual acuity and pupillary reflexes are normal.
  • Proptosis and ophthalmoplegia are pathognomonic signs of orbital cellulitis (postseptal).

items.title: Intracranial complications
items.contentHtml:
Serious complication: cavernous sinus thrombosis

  • Cavernous sinus thrombosis is the most feared complication; Clinical features: bilateral ophthalmoplegia, progressive proptosis, headache and high fever.
  • Other signs: cranial nerve paresis (III, IV, V1, V2, VI), papillary edema, meningismus.
  • Diagnosis: MRI with venography or angio-CT; Treatment: anticoagulation (heparin) plus broad-spectrum antibiotics.

items.foco: Imaging study: CT of orbits with contrast
items.title: Diagnostic imaging
items.contentHtml:
Imaging study: CT of orbits with contrast

  • The CT of orbits with contrast is the study of choice (sensitivity >90% for subperiosteal abscesses).
  • Findings: opacity of paranasal sinuses, thickening of the periosteum, displacement of the rectus muscle medial, hydense collection with peripheral enhancement (abscess).
  • MRI with gadolinium is superior to evaluate cavernous sinus thrombosis and intracranial extension.

items.title: Empirical antibiotic therapy
items.contentHtml:
Empirical treatment: ceftriaxone plus metronidazole

  • The empirical treatment of choice is ceftriaxone (50-100 mg/kg/day IV every 12-24 h) plus metronidazole (30 mg/kg/day IV every 8 h) to cover anaerobes.
  • Alternative in those allergic to beta-lactams: vancomycin (15-20 mg/kg/dose every 8-12 hours) plus metronidazole plus ceftazidime or cefepime. If MRSA is suspected, add vancomycin or linezolid; adjust according to culture and antibiogram.

items.title: Surgical criteria
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indication: subperiosteal abscess

  • The main surgical indication is the presence of subperiosteal abscess (collection between periosteum and bone) or orbital abscess.
  • Other indications: decreased visual acuity that does not improve with antibiotics, complete ophthalmoplegia, suspected foreign body, lack of response to 48 hours of IV antibiotics.
  • Procedure: transnasal endoscopic drainage (approach to the ethmoid sinus) or external orbitotomy depending on location.

items.title: Hospital management
items.contentHtml:
Entry criteria: always hospitalization

  • Any suspicion of orbital cellulitis requires hospitalization for IV antibiotic therapy and serial ophthalmological monitoring (every 4-6 hours: visual acuity, pupillary reflex, motility).
  • There is no outpatient management; Progression can be rapid (hours) towards visual loss or cavernous sinus thrombosis.
  • ICU admission criteria: compromised state of consciousness, meningeal signs, hemodynamic instability.

items.title: Prognosis and consequences
items.contentHtml:
Possible sequelae: permanent vision loss

  • Permanent vision loss occurs in 10-20% of cases if there is a therapeutic delay; mechanism: ischemic optic neuropathy due to compression or vasculitis.
  • Other sequelae: residual ophthalmoplegia, ptosis, fistula dacryocystitis, meningitis, brain abscess.
  • The visual prognosis is good (>90% complete recovery) if treatment is started. antibiotic in the first 24-48 hours and surgically drained when indicated.
